post: America turns 250 this year. The classrooms teaching that story deserve the tools to do it right. Civics Champion makes it easy to support a specific educator in your state, or give to all teachers in your state at once. Every dollar goes directly to classroom resources. Find a teacher to champion: 👉  https://bit.ly/4coYN0s  #CivicsChampion  #Teaching  #CivicEducation  #A250

2 minutes. 1 classroom moment. $300 weekly drawing. That's the Civic Star Challenge. Nothing else to it. You've had the moment: a discussion about the Declaration, an A250 activity that landed, a student who asked the question you didn't expect. That's your entry. Share it, and you're in. The final cycle runs through May 15. This week's drawing is still open. Enter:  https://bit.ly/463KXxf  #CivicStarChallenge  #America250  #CivicsTeacher  #HistoryTeachers

A student from Dunwoody High School outside Atlanta wrote to BRI's donors last year: "I can confidently say this organization has transformed my perspective on government and citizenship for the better. Your donation truly has a huge impact." That's what 6,500 educators trained in a single year looks like, up close. BRI's 2025 Annual Report is out, and behind every number is a story like that one. A teacher in Selma, Alabama, surprised at a pep rally with a national award. A Texas student who built a free tennis program for children with disabilities and won $10,000. Students from 17 states who gathered at Valley Forge to learn how to disagree well. And the work ahead – the Civics Innovation Lab, the A250 anniversary curriculum, and a student debate program going international in London – is more ambitious than anything BRI has undertaken before. See what 2025 built, and what's coming next.  https://bit.ly/4sKcdcI  #AnnualReport  #BRI  #CivicsEducation

Thomas Paine arrived in Philadelphia in 1774 with almost nothing. He had failed as a corset maker, a sailor, a school teacher, and a tax collector. He was trying his hand as a journalist. He was, by most measures, an unlikely person to change the course of a revolution. 14 months later, he published Common Sense, a 46-page pamphlet that sold 150,000 copies in a population of 2.5 million, was read aloud in taverns and army camps, and helped push the Continental Congress toward independence. George Washington said it would change everything. He was right. BRI's A250 Mini Documentary tells the full story. Watch it here:  https://bit.ly/4cbXPVr  #ThomasPaine  #AmericanHistory  #TeachingHistory  #SocialStudies  #A250

The students most likely to win the MyImpact Challenge aren't always in civics class. They're in FBLA running a school business. They're in Key Club coordinating a community service initiative. They're in FFA managing agricultural outreach. They're in Boy Scouts or Girl Scouts completing service projects for their community. They're already doing the work. Many of them just don't know there's a national contest built around recognizing exactly what they're doing. The MyImpact Challenge is open to students ages 13-19 doing charitable, entrepreneurial, or community-improvement projects anywhere in the country, at any scale. The project doesn't have to be civics-adjacent by name. It has to be real, and it has to reflect the kind of character and civic responsibility that makes communities stronger. That's a description that fits 1000s of student organizations across the country. If you work with or support any of these groups – as a teacher, advisor, or donor – the next step is worth taking. Deadline: May 3, 2026. 👉  https://bit.ly/3fyGGr3  #MyImpactChallenge  #FBLA  #KeyClub  #FFA  #CivicsEducation  #BRI

Civic knowledge among young Americans is at historic lows. The classroom time, tools, and funding to address it haven't kept pace. On April 30, we're gathering in Seattle to do something about it. The Civics Innovation Lab at the Bill of Rights Institute is hosting a forum at the Museum of History and Industry, bringing together educators, community leaders, and technology partners to surface real challenges and explore real solutions. What to expect: - Cocktail reception in the Faye G. Allen Grand Atrium - Moderated panel with regional leaders - Collaborative dinner built around local civic challenges - Fireside conversation to close April 30 | 6:30 PM | Museum of History and Industry, Seattle Space is limited. Register here:  https://bit.ly/4clcHQh  #MOHAI  #Civics  #BRI

No curriculum guide captures it. No textbook records it. The most important moments in civic education happen in real classrooms, with real students, and they disappear if no one writes them down. The Civic Star Challenge exists to change that. Share your Declaration or A250 classroom story this week. Be entered in the $300 drawing. And add your classroom to the record of what civics education looked like in America's 250th year. Enter here:  https://bit.ly/463KXxf  #CivicStarChallenge  #CivicsEducation  #America250  #TeacherVoice  #HistoryTeacher

Most civics teachers aren't asking for much. A classroom set of Pocket Constitutions. A few books that bring history to life. Civics Champion is how that happens: teachers post what they need, and donors give directly to their classroom. Over 6,500 requests fulfilled and counting. Support a teacher today: 👉  https://bit.ly/4coYN0s  #CivicsChampion  #CivicEducation  #Constitution  #Giving

The Supreme Court is weighing one of the most foundational questions in constitutional law. Your students don't have to wait for the ruling to engage it. In Trump v. Barbara, the Court is examining executive power over birthright citizenship: "All persons born or naturalized in the United States, and subject to the jurisdiction thereof, are citizens." 6 words – "subject to the jurisdiction thereof" – are doing a lot of work in that sentence. What do they mean? Who decides? And what principles should guide that interpretation? Think the Vote's latest question: Should all individuals born in the United States automatically receive citizenship? Students will encounter 2 serious constitutional traditions here: jus soli, citizenship by place of birth, upheld by the Supreme Court since U. S. v. Wong Kim Ark in 1898, and the argument that the Fourteenth Amendment's language leaves meaningful room for interpretation. The ability to reason through both positions, rather than react to them, is exactly the civic muscle this moment demands. Bring the Constitution into your classroom while the Court is still deliberating. Student submissions close April 23, share it this week. 👉  https://bit.ly/4tHKf29  #CivicsEducation  #ThinktheVote  #SCOTUS  #FourteenthAmendment  #BirthRightCitizenship  #ConstitutionalLaw  #SocialStudies  #HighSchoolTeachers  #BillofRightsInstitute
